homeContent.architectureHeading: Architecture is part of the product.
homeContent.architectureBody: Experience, data, edge delivery, communication, observability, and releases each get a clear responsibility—so the system stays understandable after launch.

homeContent.practiceHeading: Services built around the pressure your team feels.

case-studies.title: Selected work
case-studies.summary: Live sites, active product builds, enterprise systems, and the engineering decisions behind each result.
case-studies.seoDescription: Selected work by Alejandro Ortiz Corro across production websites, active product builds, business systems, enterprise delivery, and modern product engineering.

case-study:casa-roca.title: Casa Roca guest experience and conversion path
case-study:casa-roca.summary: A live hospitality site with a clear story, direct contact path, and production delivery discipline.
case-study:casa-roca.seoDescription: Casa Roca is a live hospitality website for a boutique stay in Veracruz, with clear place storytelling, direct guest inquiry paths, search-ready pages, and production release discipline.

caseStudyContent.overview: Casa Roca is the digital arrival point for a boutique stay in Roca Partida, Veracruz. It helps guests understand the place quickly, decide whether it fits their trip, and contact the business directly.

problem.title: Problem
problem.body: The business needed to communicate place, experience, and credibility while giving prospective guests a direct next step.

businessOutcome.title: Business outcome
businessOutcome.body: The production site gives Casa Roca a clear destination for guests, search traffic, brand trust, and direct inquiries.

role.title: Role
role.body: Product direction, bilingual experience planning, SEO metadata, release verification, performance care, and the complete guest-facing delivery.

constraints.title: Constraints
constraints.body: The experience had to stay fast on uneven travel connections, support two languages, make the location tangible, and keep the path to inquiry unmistakable.

architectureDecisions.title: Architecture decisions
architectureDecisions.body: Keep guest pages fast, treat bilingual content and search metadata as product concerns, and make direct contact the primary conversion path.

executionHighlights.title: Execution highlights
executionHighlights.body: The work covered hospitality positioning, localized routes, responsive art direction, production deployment, and verification on the real domain.

qualitySecurityPerformance.title: Quality, security, and performance
qualitySecurityPerformance.body: The live site uses explicit metadata, resilient layouts, optimized media, and stable pages that remain readable to guests and search engines.

publicEvidence.label: Live website
publicEvidence.description: Open the live Casa Roca website.

confidentialityNote.title: Scope note
confidentialityNote.body: This case focuses on the guest experience and production delivery.

case-study:the-barber-central.title: The Barber Central booking and operations system
case-study:the-barber-central.summary: An active booking and operations product shaped around the daily workflow of service businesses.
case-study:the-barber-central.seoDescription: The Barber Central shows active product architecture for booking, operations, payment readiness, and customer communication workflows.

caseStudyContent.overview: The Barber Central is an active product build for service businesses that need booking, availability, customer communication, and payments to feel like one system instead of a pile of tools.

problem.title: Problem
problem.body: Local service businesses often run bookings, reminders, staff operations, and payments across disconnected tools, which makes growth harder and creates avoidable manual work.

businessOutcome.title: Business outcome
businessOutcome.body: The product direction centers the daily operating workflow: book clients, manage availability, communicate clearly, and keep the business moving without stitching tools together by hand.

role.title: Role
role.body: Product architecture, workflow modeling, full-stack implementation planning, booking flow decisions, payments readiness, customer communication, and quality gates for an active build.

constraints.title: Constraints
constraints.body: Booking logic, staff availability, reminders, payments, and customer communication need to evolve together without making the daily workflow harder to operate.

architectureDecisions.title: Architecture decisions
architectureDecisions.body: Make the booking path simple, keep the operational dashboard task-first, and treat provider integrations as explicit business boundaries instead of hidden glue.

executionHighlights.title: Execution highlights
executionHighlights.body: The work covers booking IA, operations dashboard shape, provider setup, environment discipline, and manual browser QA for flows where auth, origin, and preview behavior matter.

qualitySecurityPerformance.title: Quality, security, and performance
qualitySecurityPerformance.body: The project emphasizes protected environments, explicit verification commands, browser QA, source checks, and careful handling of auth/provider configuration before public launch.

publicEvidence.label: Development preview
publicEvidence.description: Open the development landing page for The Barber Central.
publicEvidence.altText: The Barber Central development landing page

confidentialityNote.title: Scope note
confidentialityNote.body: This case follows the active product direction and release path.

case-study:nutri-plan.title: Nutri Plan client workflow system
case-study:nutri-plan.summary: A product system for client workflows, plans, dashboards, and educational content.
case-study:nutri-plan.seoDescription: Nutri Plan shows active product work around client workflows, structured plans, operational dashboards, educational content, and delivery quality.

caseStudyContent.overview: Nutri Plan connects nutrition workflows, dashboards, mobile access, educational content, and operational clarity across several product surfaces.

problem.title: Problem
problem.body: Nutrition operations need connected client records, plans, dashboards, and mobile experiences, but the domain becomes fragile when workflow state is split across unrelated tools.

businessOutcome.title: Business outcome
businessOutcome.body: The build organizes the product around real operational roles and shared backend state so client work, admin work, and mobile access can come from one model.

role.title: Role
role.body: Product workflow architecture, dashboard planning, backend state modeling, mobile coordination, quality practice, and release discipline.

constraints.title: Constraints
constraints.body: Health-related records require careful access boundaries, consistent workflow state, clear roles, and releases that do not interrupt ongoing client work.

architectureDecisions.title: Architecture decisions
architectureDecisions.body: Keep the backend as a shared source of truth, give dashboard and mobile workflows explicit responsibilities, and make environment and QA gates part of release readiness.

executionHighlights.title: Execution highlights
executionHighlights.body: The work spans landing, dashboard, admin dashboard, mobile app, Convex-backed state, route QA, preview verification, and operational parity with the reference system.

qualitySecurityPerformance.title: Quality, security, and performance
qualitySecurityPerformance.body: The project uses functional QA, route sweeps, environment separation, protected release branches, and preview validation when auth, routing, UI, or behavior changes.

publicEvidence.label: Sanitized preview
publicEvidence.description: Review the product screen and workflow notes.

confidentialityNote.title: Scope note
confidentialityNote.body: This case focuses on system responsibilities and delivery outcomes.

case-study:enterprise-systems.title: Enterprise systems at operational scale
case-study:enterprise-systems.summary: Complex product workflows, integrations, backend delivery, and release support across high-stakes operational environments.
case-study:enterprise-systems.seoDescription: Enterprise Systems covers business outcomes, architecture judgment, escalation leadership, backend delivery, and dependable release practice.

caseStudyContent.overview: Complex product and backend work across escalations, payment plans, cashback, customs workflows, integrations, and reliability inside large operational environments.

problem.title: Problem
problem.body: Large systems accumulate operational edge cases, integration pressure, and support escalations where the cost of unclear ownership is high.

businessOutcome.title: Business outcome
businessOutcome.body: Reliable delivery under pressure: untangling escalations, shipping backend workflows, and improving operational paths without creating new failure points.

role.title: Role
role.body: Backend delivery, escalation leadership, payment-plan and cashback work, customs workflow implementation, cross-team coordination, and production-minded problem solving.

constraints.title: Operating constraints
constraints.body: Scale, integration risk, data sensitivity, cross-team ownership, and production continuity shaped every decision.

architectureDecisions.title: Architecture decisions
architectureDecisions.body: Work from the business workflow backward, isolate risky integration points, keep operational contracts explicit, and favor maintainable backend paths over clever one-off fixes.

executionHighlights.title: Execution highlights
executionHighlights.body: Selected work includes payment-plan workflows, cashback implementation, customs handling, escalation ownership, and backend delivery across complex organizational contexts.

qualitySecurityPerformance.title: Quality, security, and performance
qualitySecurityPerformance.body: The work depends on careful change management, production awareness, data sensitivity, clear handoffs, and testing around workflows where mistakes become operational risk.

publicEvidence.label: Systems overview
publicEvidence.description: Review the enterprise delivery system through responsibilities, tradeoffs, and operational outcomes.

case-study:engineering-practice.title: Engineering practice for dependable releases
case-study:engineering-practice.summary: Modern agent-assisted engineering framed through planning, QA, architecture, observability, deploys, and documentation.
case-study:engineering-practice.seoDescription: Engineering Practice explains Alejandro Ortiz Corro's modern agent-assisted software delivery process across planning, QA, architecture, observability, deployment, and documentation.

caseStudyContent.overview: Engineering Practice is the operating system behind the work: structured discovery, TDD, agent-assisted implementation, browser QA, release discipline, observability, and documentation.

problem.title: Problem
problem.body: Modern software work moves quickly, but speed without architecture, tests, review, and production checks produces fragile systems and expensive surprises.

businessOutcome.title: Business outcome
businessOutcome.body: A repeatable engineering process lets projects move faster while still producing inspectable decisions, safer releases, better handoffs, and stronger confidence for business stakeholders.

role.title: Role
role.body: Technical planning, architecture review, TDD flow, agent orchestration, browser QA, release train discipline, source-quality checks, and documentation as part of delivery.

constraints.title: Constraints
constraints.body: Automation should increase useful feedback without hiding ownership, weakening review, or reducing delivery quality to tool choice.

architectureDecisions.title: Architecture decisions
architectureDecisions.body: Use clear seams, graph-backed public content, explicit environment contracts, protected branches, focused tests, and manual browser verification for visible behavior.

executionHighlights.title: Execution highlights
executionHighlights.body: The delivery system includes PR review, TDD tracers, visual checks, GitHub issue flow, release documentation, and browser QA.

qualitySecurityPerformance.title: Quality, security, and performance
qualitySecurityPerformance.body: Quality includes source checks, type checks, behavior tests, build verification, visual QA, and deployment paths that can be audited.

publicEvidence.label: Source and process
publicEvidence.description: Source, tests, documentation, and PR history show the engineering workflow behind the site.
publicEvidence.altText: Source and release process for AOHYS engineering practice

confidentialityNote.body: This case focuses on the delivery system behind dependable releases.

practice.title: Services for product systems that need to work.
practice.summary: Product systems, architecture modernization, and delivery acceleration shaped around the pressure your team needs to resolve.
practice.seoDescription: AOHYS services cover product systems, architecture modernization, and delivery acceleration for teams building dependable software.

practiceContent.heading: Product engineering services for systems that need to work.
practiceContent.deck: Bring the workflow that keeps breaking, the product that has outgrown its structure, or the release path that feels riskier than it should. We turn that pressure into a system your team can understand and operate.

services.title: Product systems
services.problem: Customer and operational work is scattered across screens, spreadsheets, manual handoffs, and unclear ownership.
services.result: A coherent product system with a clear customer path, dependable workflows, and data the team can act on.
services.engagement: A focused build or a staged product engagement, from system mapping through production delivery.

services.title: Architecture & modernization
services.problem: Legacy decisions, performance bottlenecks, or fragile integrations make each change slower and more dangerous.
services.result: A modernized path that protects critical behavior while making the system faster to understand, change, and scale.
services.engagement: Architecture review, modernization plan, or hands-on migration around the highest-leverage boundary.

services.title: Delivery acceleration
services.problem: The team can build features, but previews, QA, observability, and production releases still depend on heroics.
services.result: A visible delivery system with earlier feedback, safer releases, and clearer ownership when something fails.
services.engagement: A delivery diagnostic followed by targeted implementation across quality gates, environments, and release operations.

practiceContent.processHeading: A small, visible path from pressure to progress.

process.label: Frame
process.text: Name the business pressure, the people affected, and the outcome worth changing.

process.label: Map
process.text: Make flows, data, boundaries, risks, and tradeoffs visible before they become rework.

process.label: Build
process.text: Ship the smallest complete system slice with real states, instrumentation, and release checks.

process.label: Transfer
process.text: Leave the team with an operable system, decisions they can explain, and a next safe step.

practiceContent.relatedHeading: See the systems behind the service.

architecture.title: Architecture
architecture.summary: The engineering decisions behind experience, data, edge delivery, communication, observability, and releases.
architecture.seoDescription: Architecture notes for Alejandro Ortiz Corro's product systems: Astro and React experiences, Cloudflare delivery, Convex workflows, Resend communication, PostHog observability, and release checks.

architectureContent.heading: Architecture that stays operable after launch.
architectureContent.deck: A product stays dependable when experience, data, edge delivery, communication, observability, and release controls have clear responsibilities.
architectureContent.systemBody: Each layer has a job, an owner, and a visible connection to the next. The goal is not more technology; it is fewer hidden assumptions.

layers.label: Experience
layers.body: Interfaces that meet customers and operators where the work happens.

layers.label: Edge delivery
layers.body: Fast public delivery, media handling, and server-side boundaries close to the user.

layers.label: Product data
layers.body: Reactive application state with explicit auth, queries, mutations, and workflows.

layers.label: Communication
layers.body: Transactional messages and provider integrations that fail visibly.

layers.label: Observability
layers.body: Signals that explain behavior before a customer has to report it.

layers.label: Delivery
layers.body: A release path that separates review, preview, promotion, and verification.

architectureContent.tradeoffsHeading: Architecture is a sequence of tradeoffs.

tradeoffs.title: Progressive enhancement before spectacle
tradeoffs.reason: Motion and WebGL can add meaning, but the page still needs to communicate and convert without them.
tradeoffs.result: Semantic DOM content stays primary; the canvas becomes a responsive, optional layer.

tradeoffs.title: Reactive data without hidden ownership
tradeoffs.reason: Fast UI updates are valuable only when mutations, auth, and operational workflows remain explicit.
tradeoffs.result: Convex owns product state while page content and public routes stay in the typed content graph.

tradeoffs.title: Preview before production
tradeoffs.reason: A green build does not prove that a release works in its real environment.
tradeoffs.result: Protected branches, preview checks, and smoke verification make promotion a separate decision.

sections.title: Astro route system
sections.body: Astro owns localized marketing routes. Stable content IDs drive slugs, canonical metadata, language alternates, sitemap eligibility, and robots behavior instead of scattered page constants.

sections.title: Dashboard boundary
sections.body: The dashboard belongs on the same domain but has a different responsibility: authenticated, omitted from the sitemap, noindex, and isolated from marketing routes.

sections.title: Release Train
sections.body: Feature branches target develop first. Production moves through a separate main promotion path with Cloudflare/Wrangler checks, environment validation, and smoke verification before the canonical domain changes.

sections.title: Environment Contract
sections.body: Local, preview, and production settings are treated as an explicit contract. Provider dashboards may help setup, but durable deploy-time values should reconcile back to GitHub Environments.

sections.title: Public Content Graph
sections.body: The graph is the seam between content and routes. It owns locale variants, paths, SEO metadata, sitemap behavior, media references, and future dashboard publishing invariants.

sections.title: Provider responsibilities
sections.body: Cloudflare handles hosting and media delivery, Convex owns application state and dashboard workflows, PostHog owns analytics and errors, and Resend handles lead notifications.

architectureContent.boundaryNote: This repository is a public working sample. It does not make private client code, product code, credentials, private screenshots, or operational data public.

summary: Senior product engineer with 9+ years of experience across React, TypeScript, Next.js, product architecture, performance, SEO, and maintainable UI systems.
I have shipped across enterprise systems, startup products, healthcare workflows, e-commerce platforms, public business sites, and systems serving 30.5M+ monthly users.
My recent work goes beyond screens: Next.js as a backend surface, backend workflow delivery, escalation ownership, payment plans, cashback, customs workflows, and cross-functional product execution.
I work with agents, TDD, browser QA, release discipline, observability, and documentation so ambiguous goals become software teams can operate and improve.

highlights.label: 30.5M+ monthly users
highlights.text: Contributed to e-commerce platform work at Datazone/AutoZone with SEO, performance, React, and React Native modernization.

highlights.label: 80% load-time reduction
highlights.text: Executed major Next.js migration work at Tala Mobile, reducing load times from 3-5 seconds to under 1 second.

highlights.label: Mission-critical dispatch
highlights.text: Implemented keyboard dispatch workflows for a major US emergency response platform with zero-error tolerance requirements.

highlights.label: Working system sample
highlights.text: Built this site so technical evaluators can review SEO, i18n, content graph, QA, branch protection, and release practice.

privacy.title: Privacy
privacy.summary: How AOHYS treats contact data, analytics, errors, and private project information.
privacy.seoDescription: AOHYS privacy route explaining contact data, analytics, error reporting, private project boundaries, and public code limits.

privacyContent.heading: Privacy
privacyContent.intro: This site is public code as an engineering sample, but private client work and operational data stay private.

sections.title: Contact requests
sections.body: When you send the contact form, AOHYS uses your name, email, optional phone/company, preferred contact path, intent, and message only to respond to the request.

sections.title: Analytics and errors
sections.body: PostHog is used for explicit pageviews, selected CTA/form events, and browser error signals. Autocapture is disabled, and contact message text is not sent to analytics.

sections.title: Private project boundaries
sections.body: This repository is public for review. Private client code, credentials, operational records, and dashboard data are not public.

privacyContent.boundaryNote: The private dashboard under /dashboard is authenticated, noindexed, excluded from the sitemap, and separate from public SEO pages.
